claim: McDowell County personal income 'dropped by two-thirds in a single year after the Gary closure' (line 40)
source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, Regional Economic Accounts, Table CAINC1 (Personal Income Summary), McDowell County, WV, FIPS 54047 — total personal income (thousands of $), accessed via FRED series PI54047 (https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PI54047, BEA-sourced): 1985 = $390,734K; 1986 = $383,651K (−1.8%); 1987 = $335,034K (−12.7% vs 1986); 1988 = $333,247K; 1989 = $341,275K. Per-capita personal income, BEA CAINC1 via FRED PCPI54047: 1986 = $8,841; 1987 = $8,136 (−8.0%); recovers to $8,622 in 1988. Secondary origin of the claim: Carsten Dewitz, "The Rise and Fall of Coal in McDowell County, West Virginia," dewitzphotography.com — "In the following year alone, personal income in McDowell County decreased dramatically by two-thirds" — uncited; echoed verbatim by abandonedonline.net. Closure facts corroborated: e-WV (West Virginia Encyclopedia), "Gary" entry; Dewitz: 1986 underground-mine closure, "more than 1,200 jobs."
figure_check: CONTRADICTS. The BEA primary record shows NO two-thirds (≈−66.7%) single-year drop, nor anything close, in the entire 1980s. The largest single-year fall is 1986→1987 at −12.7% in total personal income (−8.0% per capita). From the 1985 peak ($390,734K) to the 1988 trough ($333,247K) the cumulative decline is only −14.7%; income never fell below $333M (a two-thirds drop would require ~$128M). The "two-thirds in one year" figure is off by roughly 5x and traces to a single uncited photography-blog assertion (Dewitz), not to any primary source. The underlying narrative facts are sound — 1986 closure, ~1,200 jobs lost, a real income decline concentrated in 1987 — but the magnitude is wrong. (Note: per-capita income barely moved because the loss was driven by out-migration, not a per-person income collapse — the opposite mechanism the "two-thirds" framing implies. Also flag the internal date inconsistency: Ch 2 says "late 1980s," Ch 8 says "1986.")
recommendation: CORRECT the figure. Do not ship "two-thirds in a single year" — it is contradicted by BEA CAINC1 and sourced only to an uncited blog. Replace with the BEA-grounded reality, e.g.: "Personal income in McDowell County fell about 13 percent in a single year after the closure (BEA, 1986→1987), and out-migration had cut the county's total income by roughly 15 percent from its 1985 peak." If the author wants the punchier longer-arc number, the defensible large figure is the multi-decade per-capita gap vs. the U.S., not a one-year collapse. Cite BEA Regional Economic Accounts, Table CAINC1, McDowell County (FIPS 54047). Mark date-lock-exempt (historical). Reconcile the Ch 2 ("late 1980s") / Ch 8 ("1986") date discrepancy to 1986. This gap is shared with Ch 8 line 58 — apply the same correction there.

claim: Purdue target-map four characteristics — high physical-labor-injury rates, limited healthcare infrastructure, high-prescribing-physician concentrations, economic precarity (line 208) — attributed to 'internal documents released through litigation'
source: Litigation record (provenance the book invokes): (1) GAO-04-110, "Prescription Drugs: OxyContin Abuse and Diversion and Efforts to Address the Problem" (Dec 2003, released Jan 2004), https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/GAOREPORTS-GAO-04-110/html/GAOREPORTS-GAO-04-110.htm — confirms Purdue "directed its sales representatives to focus on the physicians in their sales territories who were high opioid prescribers" (factor 3), but EXPLICITLY declined to link marketing to abuse geography ("we could not assess the relationship between the increased availability of OxyContin and locations where it is being abused and diverted"). (2) Commonwealth of Massachusetts v. Purdue, unsealed complaint Jan 2019, https://www.mass.gov/doc/purdue-complaint-filed/download — high-prescriber targeting + targeting "vulnerable" populations. (3) DOJ/McKinsey resolution + state suits re "Evolve to Excellence"/"Turbocharge," targeting "High Value Prescribers" and "vulnerable and underserved" populations, https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/justice-department-announces-resolution-criminal-and-civil-investigations-mckinsey-companys. Secondary/academic ground for the geographic-distress overlay (the factors NOT in litigation docs as a map): (4) Patrick Radden Keefe, Empire of Pain (Doubleday, 2021) — documents IMS prescriber-profiling, rep targeting of high-workplace-injury regions and fewer-doctor rural Appalachia (factors 1, 2, 3). (5) Alpert, Evans, Lieber & Powell, "Origins of the Opioid Crisis and Its Enduring Impacts," NBER WP 26500 / Quarterly Journal of Economics 2022, https://www.nber.org/papers/w26500 — triplicate-state natural experiment showing marketing intensity drove overdose geography (causal link GAO would not assert). (6) S. Monnat, "Diseases of Despair"/ARC Appalachian Diseases of Despair (Aug 2017), https://www.arc.gov/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/AppalachianDiseasesofDespairAugust2017.pdf — economic distress / disability / mining-collapse as predictors of opioid mortality (factors 1, 4).
figure_check: CORRECTS the provenance claim. The litigation documents do NOT contain a single unified four-characteristic Purdue "target map" (injury + healthcare scarcity + prescriber concentration + economic precarity as one internal targeting framework). What the litigation record actually documents is narrower: prescriber-level targeting of high-volume prescribers (factor 3, GAO + MA + McKinsey) and rhetorical targeting of "vulnerable/underserved" populations (McKinsey). GAO affirmatively declined to map marketing onto abuse/distress geography. The other three factors (physical-labor-injury rates, limited healthcare infrastructure, economic precarity) as a deliberate geographic-selection grid are established by Keefe's reporting (workplace-injury regions; fewer-doctor rural areas) and by peer-reviewed economic geography (Alpert et al. causal link; Monnat distress predictors) — NOT by an internal Purdue exhibit. So the four-factor map is a sound SYNTHESIS, but its wholesale attribution to "internal documents released through litigation" overstates the documentary record.
recommendation: Soften the provenance and split the citation rather than cut. The claim is defensible but currently over-attributes a synthesized framework to a single category of source. Suggested revision: keep "internal documents — released through litigation" for the prescriber-targeting + vulnerable-population pieces (factor 3 + the "vulnerable/underserved" framing — solidly litigation-documented via GAO-04-110, the MA complaint, and the McKinsey E2E/"Turbocharge" record), but re-attribute the geographic-distress overlay (injury rates, healthcare scarcity, economic precarity as a county-selection logic) to Keefe's Empire of Pain (2021) for the reporting and to the peer-reviewed economic geography (Alpert/Evans/Lieber/Powell, NBER 26500/QJE 2022; Monnat / ARC Diseases of Despair 2017) for the causal/correlational structure. Recommended framing: "...the litigation record documents that Purdue's sales force was directed at high-volume prescribers and explicitly courted 'vulnerable and underserved' populations; reporting (Keefe) and subsequent economic-geography research show how closely the resulting promotional map tracked counties marked by high physical-labor-injury rates, thin healthcare infrastructure, and economic precarity." Avoid the implication that Purdue drew a single internal four-factor county map "itself" — that specific exhibit was not verifiable. Flag line 210's "a target map a single company drew itself" for the same softening, since it inherits the same over-attribution.

claim: Norwegian extraction pace is 'deliberately limited, despite recurring industry pressure to accelerate.'
source: PRIMARY: St.meld. nr. 25 (1973-74), "Petroleumsvirksomhetens plass i det norske samfunn" / "The role of the petroleum industry in Norwegian society," Norwegian Ministry of Finance (Bratteli government), presented February 1974 — established that Norway should "maintain a moderate pace in recovering the petroleum resources" (a "moderat tempo"), operationally defined as 50-90 million tonnes of oil equivalent (~375-675 million boe) per year. SCHOLARLY: Helge Ryggvik / "Norway: Public Debate and the Management of Petroleum Resources and Revenues," in The Palgrave Handbook of the International Political Economy of Energy / Springer (2017), https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-319-60627-9_13 — documents the "go slow" / moderate-pace doctrine, that a Conservative finance spokesman's proposal to slow petroleum development "was met by a 'storm of protest' from the oil companies," and that the sovereign-wealth-fund era ended the self-imposed limitations. CORROBORATING: Yale SOM Equinor case "Norwegian Oil Policy" (Ten Oil Commandments, 1971); Equinor/Norwegian Petroleum Museum industriminne.no on the 1982 Tempo Commission and the 1987-88 development "queue system" (Shell reacting sharply over the Draugen project); Offshore Norge official petroleum history ("The Storting wanted a moderate pace of exploration and opened the NCS gradually").
figure_check: MATCHES. The book's characterization is directly supported. "Deliberately limited" maps to the 1974 White Paper No. 25 explicit policy of a "moderate pace" of recovery (50-90 Mtoe/yr cap, a level not reached until the late 1980s) — a deliberate, parliament-endorsed restraint, not a market outcome. "Recurring industry pressure to accelerate" maps to documented episodes: the Conservative slowdown proposal "met by a 'storm of protest' from the oil companies," Shell's sharp resistance to the 1987-88 development queue system over Draugen, and the eventual abandonment of the moderate-pace commitment as sector clout grew ("vested interests within the sector started pushing harder for expansion"). Both halves of the compound claim are sourced.
recommendation: Drop in a citation. Recommended: cite St.meld. nr. 25 (1973-74) as the primary anchor for the "deliberately limited / moderate pace" doctrine, and Ryggvik's Springer chapter (2017) for the industry-pressure / "storm of protest" dimension. A clean footnote: 'Norway's "moderate pace" doctrine dates to White Paper No. 25 (1973-74), which set a deliberate annual recovery ceiling; industry repeatedly pressed to accelerate (e.g., the "storm of protest" that met a Conservative slowdown proposal; Shell's resistance to the 1987-88 development queue), and the restraint was eventually relaxed.' No hedge needed — the characterization is well attested. Optional: note in passing that the policy was later loosened, so the present-tense "is deliberately limited" is most defensible if framed as the long-standing tradition/doctrine rather than a claim about current (post-1990s) production management.

claim: Revenue routing: much of Nigeria's oil revenue flowed through Shell, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Total into financial systems outside Nigeria, and a substantial fraction reaching the state was 'captured by political elites in Abuja, Lagos, and the international accounts routinely documented in subsequent corruption prosecutions.'
source: Three-pronged corroboration for a synthetic narrative claim: (1) Revenue routed offshore via the named majors: SOMO (Centre for Research on Multinational Corporations), "How Shell, Total and Eni benefit from tax breaks in Nigeria's gas industry," 2016 (tax-incentive/transfer-pricing structures shifting Nigerian gas revenue to the majors); Shell plc tax-transparency disclosures (Shell reported $5.34bn paid to Nigeria in 2024 — company-as-conduit posture); NEITI review of 26 oil blocks / $6.03bn divestments by five IOCs (2024). (2) Elite capture of state-reaching revenue: Sanusi Lamido Sanusi (then CBN Governor), 2013-14 letter/Senate testimony on ~$20bn unremitted NNPC oil revenue (disputed; NNPC conceded a $1.48bn refund Jan 2015); Oby Ezekwesili (former World Bank VP for Africa), 52nd NBA AGM, Abuja, Aug 2012 ($400bn lost since 1960, corroborated by UNODC ~$400bn 1960-1999); Matthew Page, "A New Taxonomy for Corruption in Nigeria," Carnegie Endowment, July 2018. (3) Documented in prosecutions: Abacha asset recovery — ~$3.65-5bn recovered across U.S. DOJ forfeiture ($311m + $23m, 2020), Switzerland (>$1bn), UK/Jersey (>$300m); OPL 245 / Malabu bribery affair — Shell + Eni $1.3bn purchase, ~$1.1bn alleged channeled to Nigerian officials incl. ex-minister Dan Etete (Milan trial, EFCC charges, UK civil suit, US/Netherlands investigations).
figure_check: No numeric figure in the claim to check; it is a qualitative characterization. Found sources MATCH every component: (a) majors as offshore-routing conduits (SOMO 2016, Shell disclosures, NEITI 2024), (b) "substantial fraction captured by political elites" (Sanusi $20bn / NNPC $1.48bn concession; Ezekwesili/UNODC $400bn; Carnegie taxonomy), (c) "routinely documented in subsequent corruption prosecutions" (Abacha recoveries across US/Swiss/UK courts; OPL 245/Malabu Milan + EFCC + UK prosecutions). No component contradicted. Caveat: Milan court ACQUITTED Shell/Eni (definitive March 2021) and Nigerian courts discharged Etete/Adoke (March 2024) — prosecutions occurred and are documented but did not end in convictions of the corporates; "documented in prosecutions" remains accurate.
recommendation: Keep the sentence; defensible and accurate as a synthetic characterization, but because it names four corporations and alleges elite capture it should carry a footnote rather than stand bare. Recommended drop-in cite cluster: SOMO 2016 (offshore-routing-via-majors) + Sanusi 2013-14 / NEITI (state-revenue capture) + Abacha asset-recovery record (US DOJ + Swiss + UK/Jersey) + OPL 245/Malabu prosecution record (anchors "documented in subsequent corruption prosecutions"). One precision edit for fairness: OPL 245 corporate defendants were acquitted in Milan (2021) and Nigerian defendants discharged (2024) — keep "documented in...corruption prosecutions" but avoid any phrasing implying convictions of the named majors. The chapter's broader framing ("routed revenue outside Nigeria... international architecture") is fully supported; the elite-capture clause is the one to footnote most carefully.

claim: International-architecture passage: tax-treaty structures legitimizing transfer-pricing into low-disclosure jurisdictions, financial-secrecy intermediation, concession terms inherited from pre-independence arrangements (Pistor/Code-of-Capital seam).
source: All three components are externally grounded; the passage just needs the cites attached. (1) Transfer-pricing into low-disclosure jurisdictions: Tax Justice Network, "Tackling Profit Shifting in the Oil and Gas Sector for a Just Transition" (2025), https://taxjustice.net/2025/11/05/ — extractive firms shift ~34% of true profits to low-tax jurisdictions via marketing hubs and offshore shells; TJN, "Profit shifting by multinational corporations: Evidence from transaction-level data in Nigeria" (Working Paper 2024-01), https://taxjustice.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/TJN-WP-2024-01-...pdf (Nigeria-specific). (2) Financial-secrecy intermediation + home-regulator non-liability: TJN, "Capital flight from Africa" (2021), https://taxjustice.net/2021/03/24/ (Nigeria $467B capital flight 1970-2018; worst secrecy culprits named as UK + territories, US, Switzerland, Netherlands) + Global Financial Integrity, "Secrecy Jurisdictions," https://gfintegrity.org/issue/secrecy-jurisdictions/. (3) Concession terms inherited from pre-independence arrangements: Human Rights Watch, "The Price of Oil" / "Oil and Natural Gas in Nigeria" (1999), https://www.hrw.org/reports/1999/nigeria/Nigew991-03.htm (1914 Crown ordinance; 1938 Shell D'Arcy monopoly; 1946 Shell-BP; 1960 independence; 1969 Petroleum Decree) + Natural Resource Governance Institute, "Nigeria — 1969 Petroleum Act." The interpretive frame: Katharina Pistor, The Code of Capital: How the Law Creates Wealth and Inequality (Princeton UP, 2019), esp. Ch. 6 "A Code for the Globe" (pp. 132-157) — jurisdictional arbitrage / "law shopping," offshore conduits that transform flow characteristics, and the UK/US-as-rule-makers vs. rule-takers structure.
figure_check: MATCHES. No numeric figure is asserted in the passage, so there is nothing to correct. Each qualitative mechanism named in the sentence is independently corroborated: (a) transfer-pricing into low-disclosure jurisdictions — TJN documents this for extractives generally and Nigeria specifically; (b) financial-secrecy intermediation with home regulators declining extraterritorial liability — matches TJN/GFI secrecy-jurisdiction reporting and Pistor's rule-maker/rule-taker thesis (the very jurisdictions hosting the operating-company affiliates are the rule-makers who decline to extend liability); (c) concession terms inherited from pre-independence arrangements made before the Nigerian state existed as counterparty — exactly matches the documented 1938 Shell D'Arcy colonial monopoly → 1946 Shell-BP → 1960 independence → 1969 Petroleum Decree chronology. The PRIOR NOTE's framing ("not web-resolvable as a single fact") is correct that it is not ONE discrete fact, but each of its three load-bearing assertions is web-resolvable and all three check out.
recommendation: RETAIN and develop with citation (do not cut). The author's instinct here is well-founded — nothing is invented and the characterization is accurate on all three axes. Concrete fix: attach a single endnote covering the seam. Recommended note text: cite Katharina Pistor, The Code of Capital (2019), Ch. 6 "A Code for the Globe," for the legal-architecture frame (jurisdictional arbitrage; offshore conduits; UK/US rule-makers vs. rule-takers); cite Tax Justice Network's oil-and-gas profit-shifting reporting (2025) plus its Nigeria transaction-level working paper (2024-01) for the transfer-pricing/secrecy mechanism; and cite Human Rights Watch (1999) and/or the Natural Resource Governance Institute on the 1969 Petroleum Act for the inherited-concession lineage (1938 Shell D'Arcy → 1946 Shell-BP → 1969 Decree). One optional tightening: the phrase "concession terms inherited from pre-independence arrangements made when the Nigerian state did not yet exist as a counterparty" is rhetorically strong; the 1969 Petroleum Decree did re-vest ownership in the post-independence state, so consider adding a half-clause acknowledging that the post-1969 state renegotiated terms but operated on infrastructure and incumbency advantages first granted under colonial concessions — this preserves the (accurate) point that the originating grants predate the Nigerian counterparty without overstating that the legal terms were never revisited.

claim: Libby, MT documented costs 'exceed four billion dollars' against ~$100M lifetime revenue (40:1 cost-to-revenue ratio).
source: Authoritative LIBBY-SPECIFIC documented public figures (multiple strategies, all corroborated): (1) EPA Superfund cleanup ~$600M over 19 years, the largest asbestos cleanup in U.S. history — EPA Libby Asbestos Site profile + GAO-03-469 (2003) + asbestos.com 2018-12-11. (2) W.R. Grace 2008 cleanup-cost settlement $250M — DOJ press release #08-194 (2008-03-11). (3) Montana natural-resource-damages settlement $18.5M (2023) — Montana DOJ NRDP + Gov. Gianforte release. (4) State failure-to-warn settlements totaling $68M to 2,000+ residents (2011 + 2017) — asbestos.com. (5) BNSF railyard wrongful-death verdicts $4M each (Wells/Walder), overturned on appeal 2026 — Montana Free Press 2026-02-24. COMPANY-WIDE (not Libby-specific) $4B figures that likely seeded the book number: W.R. Grace asbestos PI+PD trusts funded with "more than $4 billion" under §524(g) covering all ~325,000 national claims — WRG Asbestos PI Trust (wrgraceasbestostrust.com) + SEC EX1001-PITrustAgreement + Grace 2008 Joint Plan of Reorganization; and Sen. Baucus's 2002 statement that Grace "hide[d] at least $4 billion in assets" (assets shielded, not costs incurred) — Senate hearing CHRG-107shrg83698.
figure_check: CONTRADICTS as written. No authoritative source documents $4B in LIBBY-specific societal/health/cleanup/settlement costs. Summing the documented Libby-specific public figures (~$600M cleanup + $250M Grace settlement + $18.5M NRD + $68M failure-to-warn + scattered verdicts) yields roughly $0.9-1.0B — an order of magnitude below $4B, implying a defensible cost-to-revenue ratio of ~9-10:1 on the documented public record, not 40:1. The two real "$4 billion" figures both attach to the WRONG referent: (a) the §524(g) PI+PD trusts ("more than $4 billion") cover Grace's ENTIRE national asbestos liability across ~325,000 claims, the overwhelming majority unrelated to Libby; (b) Baucus's $4B is assets Grace SHIELDED, not costs the disaster IMPOSED. The ~$100M Libby lifetime-revenue denominator is internally stated and plausible but itself uncited. So the 40:1 ratio rests on a numerator that, where it can be sourced at all, is a company-wide-liability or shielded-assets figure mis-attributed to Libby-specific documented costs.
recommendation: Do not ship "$4 billion documented Libby costs / 40:1" as a documented figure — it conflates company-wide Grace asbestos liability (or shielded assets) with Libby-specific severed costs, and the spot-fix matters because the book uses Libby as a flagship cost-severance proof. Three honest options, in order of preference: (1) REFRAME as a book-derived order-of-magnitude estimate with disclosed components: state the documented Libby-specific public figures (~$600M EPA cleanup; $250M Grace cleanup settlement [DOJ #08-194]; $18.5M NRD; $68M failure-to-warn settlements; ~400-694 deaths and ~2,400 diagnoses whose lifetime medical + lost-life-years + property-value costs are NOT in those settlement figures), then state that a full societal-cost accounting that monetizes the uncompensated mortality, morbidity, and property loss plausibly reaches the low billions — labeled explicitly as the framework's estimate, not a documented total. This keeps the cost-severance point (documented public spend is a fraction of true cost) while dropping the unsupported precise "$4 billion / 40:1." (2) If the author wants to retain a $4B-scale figure, RE-ANCHOR it correctly: cite the §524(g) trusts' "more than $4 billion" as Grace's TOTAL asbestos liability (court-confirmed, wrgraceasbestostrust.com / 2008 Joint Plan) and note Libby was the most lethal single node within it — but then the ~$100M denominator must change to Grace's company-wide revenue, breaking the clean Libby 40:1. (3) DEMOTE to the documented public record only: ~$870M+ in documented public spend/settlements against ~$100M Libby revenue ≈ a documented ~9:1, with the true ratio stated as "far higher once uncompensated mortality and morbidity are priced." Recommend option (1): it is the most defensible and is consistent with the book's own method (price the residual cost the legal architecture severed) while honestly flagging the aggregate as a derived estimate.

claim: Deepwater Horizon: $150 billion total economic losses (aggregate from academic, insurance-industry, and federal sources) — the denominator of the 40% recovery ratio.
source: CLOSEST REAL SOURCE: Gyo Lee (2018), "Ultimate Costs of the Disaster: Seven Years After the Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill," Journal of Corporate Accounting & Finance 29(1):69-79, DOI 10.1002/jcaf.22306 — ultimate cost TO BP = US$144.89 billion (vs. $62.59B on BP's income statement), of which ~$61.41B is "hidden costs" (securities/market-cap/reputational, "not reportable"). SUPPORTING COMPONENT SOURCES: NRDA natural-resource damage $17.2B — Bishop et al. (2017), Science 356(6335):eaam8525, "Putting a value on injuries to natural assets: The BP oil spill" (Virginia Tech / NOAA NRDA). Fisheries 7-yr present-value loss ~$8.7B — Sumaila et al. (2012), Can. J. Fish. Aquat. Sci. 69(3):499-510. Tourism "could exceed ~$23B over 3 years" — U.S. Travel Association (2010), Oxford Economics projection. Housing/property capitalized loss $3.8-5.0B — Winkler & Gordon (2019), J. Housing Economics. BP direct all-in cost ~$61.6B final estimate (BP, June 2016) / >$65B (Wikipedia, Maritime Executive).
figure_check: CORRECTS-AND-MISMATCHES. No published source produces a "$150B total economic losses to the Gulf, aggregated from academic + insurance + federal sources." The nearest figure, Lee (2018) $144.89B, (a) rounds to ~$145B not "over $150B," and (b) measures the wrong thing — "ultimate cost TO BP" (including ~$61B of securities/reputational/market-cap "hidden costs" borne by BP shareholders), NOT total economic losses absorbed by the Gulf region. Summing the documented regional-loss components (NRDA $17.2B + fisheries $8.7B + tourism up-to-$23B + housing $3.8-5.0B + BP direct costs ~$65B) reaches only ~$100-115B and double-counts BP-paid amounts against third-party damages. Two further problems: the book's own Ch6 prices Deepwater total cost at $65-70B (IPG 15-17x) — Ch5's $150B is ~2.2x the book's own figure for the same event; and the book's component sub-claims of "$12B property-value decline" and "tourism losses exceeded $7B" are not matched by sources (housing literature shows $3.8-5.0B; tourism sources give a high-end ~$23B projection, not a settled $7B). The numerator is also unstable: line 50 says "$60 billion in penalties" while line 46 says BP's $21B fines + $20B fund + total ~$65B documented cost.
recommendation: Do NOT present $150B as a sourced "total economic losses" aggregate — no such figure exists and the framing ("academic, insurance-industry, and federal sources") overstates the record. Three options, in order of preference: (1) RE-ANCHOR to Lee (2018) and relabel honestly: "The most comprehensive academic accounting (Lee 2018, J. Corp. Acct. & Fin.) puts BP's ultimate all-in cost at roughly $145 billion — more than double the $62.6B on BP's income statement — once securities, legal, and reputational charges are included." Then compute the recovery ratio against a cost CONCEPT the source actually supports, and note it is cost-to-BP, not total Gulf loss. (2) REPLACE the single $150B denominator with a DEFENDED RANGE built from cited components (NRDA $17.2B [Bishop 2017] + tourism up-to-$23B [US Travel Assoc.] + fisheries $8.7B [Sumaila 2012] + housing $3.8-5.0B [Winkler-Gordon 2019] + BP direct ~$65B), explicitly labeled a book-derived aggregation showing its inputs, yielding a range (~$100-145B) rather than a false-precision $150B; the 40% ratio then becomes a range (~42-60%). (3) RECONCILE with Ch6: if the book elsewhere uses $65-70B total cost (IPG 15-17x), either Ch5 must adopt the same denominator or the chapters must explicitly flag the two figures as different methodological lenses (one-event bounded cost vs. comprehensive ultimate cost). Also fix the numerator: line 50's "$60 billion in penalties" should be reconciled to line 46's ~$41-65B. Finally, correct or drop the unsupported "$12B property decline" and "$7B tourism" sub-figures, replacing with the sourced $3.8-5.0B housing and the US Travel Assoc. tourism projection.

claim: Deepwater Horizon: tourism revenue losses >$7B over three years; property-value declines ~$12B.
source: TOURISM: Oxford Economics, "Potential Impact of the Gulf Oil Spill on Tourism" (prepared for the U.S. Travel Association, July 21, 2010). Two NOAA-oil-flow-probability scenarios: LOW-impact = $7.6 billion in lost tourism revenue over ~15 months; HIGH-impact = $22.7 billion over ~3 years. Reported: CNN Travel, "Gulf spill could cost region $22.7 billion in travel dollars," July 22, 2010 (cnn.com/2010/TRAVEL/07/22/gulf.oil.travel.impact/); eTurboNews, July 2010; House hearing CHRG-111hhrg78127. PROPERTY: best authoritative estimates are (a) Cano-Urbina, Clapp & Willardsen, "The effects of the BP Deepwater Horizon oil spill on housing markets," Journal of Housing Economics 43 (2019): 131-156 — 4-8% home-price decline capitalizing $3.8-5.0 billion in damage; and (b) CoreLogic, "New CoreLogic Data Shows the Potential Impact..." (PRNewswire, June 2010) — $3 billion over five years for the 15 already-affected coastal counties, with a worst-case $28 billion over five years ONLY IF oil reached the Florida Keys/Atlantic coast (it did not).
figure_check: TOURISM ($7B/3yr): CORRECTS the time horizon. The $7-billion magnitude is real and sourceable — it is the Oxford Economics LOW-impact scenario of $7.6 billion — but that figure covers ~15 months, NOT "the three years following the spill." The three-year horizon belongs to the $22.7 billion HIGH scenario. So the book conflates the low-scenario dollar figure with the high-scenario time frame. (The prior note's worry that $7B is "inconsistent with the $23B" is resolved: they are the two endpoints of one Oxford Economics study, not rival sources.) PROPERTY ($12B): CONTRADICTS / unsupported. No authoritative source produces ~$12B in property-value decline. Peer-reviewed capitalization is $3.8-5.0B; CoreLogic base case is $3B over five years; the only number near $12B in the literature is unrelated (≈$12B of restoration funds remaining through 2032, per NOAA). The $28B CoreLogic figure is a counterfactual worst case that did not occur.
recommendation: TOURISM — drop-in fix with corrected horizon: replace "Tourism revenue losses in affected areas exceeded seven billion dollars in the three years following the spill" with the sourced framing, e.g. "Oxford Economics, analyzing for the U.S. Travel Association, projected Gulf tourism losses between $7.6 billion (lower-flow scenario, ~15 months) and $22.7 billion (higher-flow scenario, ~3 years)." This both pins the figure and removes the reviewer-exposure inconsistency. PROPERTY — do NOT keep $12B; it is not externally supportable. Either (a) replace with the peer-reviewed figure: "A 2019 Journal of Housing Economics study (Cano-Urbina, Clapp & Willardsen) estimated coastal housing markets capitalized $3.8-5.0 billion in spill damage," or (b) cut the property-value sentence. Note the downstream effect: lowering tourism+property inputs does NOT threaten the chapter's "~$150B total / 40% recovery" headline, which rests on the separately documented $65B BP cost and the aggregate-loss claim — but the aggregate ">$150 billion" line on the same page (line 48) should itself be re-sourced in a separate pass, since these two sub-figures were apparently feeding reader confidence in it.

claim: Baotou: ~60% of world rare earths; ~10 tons wastewater per ton rare-earth oxide; remediation costs 'will exceed one hundred billion dollars'; industry spends <1% of revenue on remediation; $10-12B annual Chinese rare-earth revenue (Baotou ~half).
source: (a) SHARE — Schreiber et al. (2022/2016 review), "Environmental impacts of rare earth production," MRS Bulletin 47:309–317 / PMC8929459: "China represents the largest REEs producer worldwide with 140,000 t REO equivalents in 2020 (58%)." USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries: China ~69.2% of world mine production (2024). USGS, "China's rare-earth industry," OFR 2011-1042. (b) WASTEWATER — Schreiber et al. flotation tailings "up to 40 t/t REO"; Chinese Society of Rare Earths figures (via Harvard Int'l Review; Science News, "Rare earth mining... at what cost?"): per ton REO ~200 m³ acidic wastewater, ~9,600–12,000 m³ waste gas, ~1.4 t radioactive residue, ~2,000 t total toxic waste; Wang et al. (2017), "Evaluation of Externalities Associated with Rare Earth Exploitation at Bayan Obo," external cost CNY 34,739/t REO (2013). (c) REMEDIATION TOTAL — NO source found at $100B. Closest authoritative: China MIIT 2019 estimate ~$5.5B (¥38B) to clean up illegal Jiangxi sites (Yale E360, "China Wrestles with the Toxic Aftermath of Rare Earth Mining"); Baotou central-gov remediation actually distributed ~$43M (2015, Yale E360). (d/e) REVENUE & COST — Zhang, T., Zhang, P., Peng, K., Feng, K., Fang, P., Chen, W., Zhang, N., Wang, P., & Li, J. (2022), "Allocating environmental costs of China's rare earth production to global consumption," Science of the Total Environment 831:154934, doi:10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.154934 — total annual environmental cost of China's REE production ~$7.4B (2010) to ~$7.2B (2015); foreign consumption $4.8B (2010)→$5.4B (2015). Zhou & Ge (2021), "Estimating the environmental cost of mixed rare earth production with willingness to pay: A case study in Baotou," The Extractive Industries and Society 8(1):340–354 — WTP ~CNY 153.63/person/yr, total ~CNY 442M/yr. China Northern Rare Earth (Baotou producer) H1-2023 revenue ~RMB 9.13B (~$1.4B); 2018 revenue RMB 13.85B (~$2B).
figure_check: (a) MATCHES if read as China's share (USGS ~69%; Schreiber 58% in 2020) — but the book attributes it to "Baotou," and Baotou/Bayan Obo is ~half of China's output (~30–35% of world). As written, Baotou-specific "~60%" is too high; as China's share it is right. Must disambiguate. (b) MATCHES and is CONSERVATIVE — literature reports far higher waste burdens per ton (flotation tailings up to 40 t/t; ~200 m³ acidic wastewater/t; "dozens to hundreds of tons" of contaminated water per ton). "~10 tons wastewater/t" is defensibly low-end. (c) CONTRADICTS / UNSUPPORTED — no source reaches $100B. Authoritative figures: annual all-China environmental cost ~$7.4B (Zhang 2022); largest cleanup estimate $5.5B (MIIT, Jiangxi, not Baotou); Baotou outlay $43M. A $100B cumulative figure would need an explicit centuries-horizon NPV derivation, which no source provides. HIGHEST-RISK. (d) PARTIAL — "$10–12B China REE revenue" plausibly conflates mining-stage oxide revenue with full industry value-chain output (Baotou City's 2025 target was ¥100B≈$14B for the whole industry incl. magnets/alloys). Single dominant Baotou producer revenue ~$2–2.8B/yr, so "Baotou ~half of $10–12B = $5–6B" is on the high side for the producer but plausible for the broader Baotou cluster. (e) UNVERIFIED — no source quantifies industry remediation spend as "<1% of revenue"; directionally consistent with TEA-underinvestment literature but the specific percentage is uncorroborated.
recommendation: SPLIT the bundle. (a) Disambiguate: change to "China produces roughly sixty to seventy percent of the world's rare earth elements [USGS]; the Bayan Obo deposit feeding Baotou is about half of that output" — do not attribute ~60% world share to Baotou alone. (b) KEEP and strengthen with a cite (Schreiber et al., MRS Bulletin 2022, flotation tailings up to 40 t/t REO; or Chinese Society of Rare Earths figures via Science News) and optionally note the book's ~10 t is a deliberately conservative low-end. (c) DEMOTE the $100B figure — it is genuinely unsourceable. Either (i) cut it, (ii) replace with sourced figures (annual environmental cost ~$7B, Zhang et al. 2022; MIIT $5.5B Jiangxi cleanup), or (iii) if the author intends a cumulative centuries-horizon estimate, label it explicitly as a book-derived estimate and SHOW the inputs (annual externality × horizon × discount), per the internal-derivation discipline — but as a bare assertion it will not survive a reviewer. (d) Soften/cite: anchor "$10–12B" to whether it means oxide revenue or full industry output value, and tie Baotou's share to China Northern Rare Earth disclosures. (e) Either source the "<1%" claim to a specific company-disclosure/TEA analysis or soften to "a small fraction." CITATION-THEATER FIX: replace the bare three-journal name-drop in line 68 with the actual papers now identified — Zhang et al. 2022 (Sci Total Environ); Zhou & Ge 2021 (Extractive Industries & Society); plus the Baotou children's-health studies (2003 intellectual-development; 2017 urinary rare-earth biomarkers) — so the named journals carry real cites rather than reading as decorative.

claim: Social Security: $108 trillion foregone-return figure ('largest cost severance in human history') — equity-index counterfactual.
source: No external source produces $108T. The authoritative counterfactual is Gale, Krupkin & Rueben, "How would investing in equities have affected the Social Security trust fund?" (Brookings / Center for Retirement Research at Boston College, WP 2016-6): a 40%-equity allocation phased in from 1984 would have left reserves of $3.8T vs. actual $2.8T at end-2015 — a ~$1.0T gap. Bounding inputs: SSA OASDI trust-fund reserves peaked at ~$2.91T (2021), ~$2.7T end-2024 (2024 OASDI Trustees Report; SSA Trust Fund data). Cumulative TOTAL program income from all sources 1937–2009 was ~$13.8T (justfacts.com compiling SSA history tables); annual income is ~$1.35T (2023), giving rough cumulative 1937–2024 income of ~$28–32T. The largest equity-invested sovereign wealth fund on Earth (Norway GPFG) holds ~$2T after 30 years (Wikipedia, Government Pension Fund of Norway, May 2026). S&P 500 long-run CAGR ~10.2–10.4% with dividends reinvested (NYU Stern / slickcharts).
figure_check: CONTRADICTS. The nearest authoritative external value (Brookings, ~$1T gap on a 40%-equity-from-1984 counterfactual) is two orders of magnitude below $108T. More decisively, $108T is mathematically unreachable even as a generous internal derivation from the book's own stated method (reinvesting the surpluses in a broad equity index): it is ~37x the actual trust-fund peak (~$2.9T), ~3.5x the entire GROSS cumulative income of the program across 88 years (~$28–32T, almost all of which was paid out as benefits the same year under pay-as-you-go and never accumulated), and ~54x the world's largest equity-invested sovereign fund (~$2T). The equity-vs-bond return differential (~10.4% vs ~5–6%) compounds to roughly 3–6x over the longest holding windows, not 37x. The figure is also internally inconsistent with the chapter's own line 100 ("tens of trillions of dollars in unfunded obligations," consistent with the Trustees' ~$22.6T 75-year present-value shortfall).
recommendation: Do NOT present $108T as a documented figure, and do not present it as a defensible internal derivation — it fails the upper-bound reconstruction from its own named inputs. Likely basis: an author/model calculation that compounds a full-equity return on a mis-scaled principal base (e.g., applying ~88 years of equity compounding to cumulative gross contributions or to an annual-income stream rather than to the actual accumulated surplus, and/or double-counting by not netting benefit payouts under pay-as-you-go). Recommended fixes, in order of preference: (1) CUT the $108T and the "largest cost severance in human history" claim entirely — the structural argument in lines 134–146 (severance is in the obligation-generation mechanism, not the portfolio) stands on its own and is actually stronger without a fragile headline number; (2) if a number is wanted, REPLACE with the sourced Brookings/CRR counterfactual (~$1T foregone on a 40%-equity-from-1984 path) explicitly cited, or with the Trustees' ~$22.6T 75-year present-value actuarial shortfall, labeled as such; (3) only if the author insists on retaining an own-model figure, DEMOTE it to an explicitly labeled illustrative counterfactual with full methodology disclosure as a footnoted band (return index + period + contribution/drawdown schedule + real vs. nominal + treatment of pay-as-you-go outflows) AND reconcile it against the trust-fund-peak and cumulative-income ceilings above, which the current $108T violates. Note the internal contradiction with line 100 must be resolved regardless of which option is chosen.

claim: The United States is the only wealthy nation whose healthcare-financing architecture produces the GoFundMe-as-default pattern at this scale (line 166).
source: PRIMARY COMPARATIVE SOURCE (found now): Saleh SN, Ajufo E, Lehmann CU, Medford RJ. "A Comparison of Online Medical Crowdfunding in Canada, the UK, and the US." JAMA Network Open. 2020;3(10):e2021684. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2020.21684. (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7588935/) — 3,396 GoFundMe campaigns Feb 2018–Mar 2019: US median raised $38,204 vs. Canada $12,662 vs. UK $6,285; campaigns for ROUTINE treatment were 77.9% of US treatment campaigns vs. 21.9% (Canada) and 26.6% (UK), the authors attributing the US pattern to "high out-of-pocket costs associated with essential health care." SUPPORTING: Kenworthy N, Igra M. "Medical Crowdfunding and Disparities in Health Care Access in the United States, 2016‒2020." American Journal of Public Health. 2022;112(3):491–498 (437,596 US campaigns; $2B+ from 21.7M donations; 12% met goals, 16% zero donations) — already cited in the book. Renwick MJ, Mossialos E. "Crowdfunding our health: Economic risks and benefits." Social Science & Medicine. 2017;191:48–56. Germany context: Bassani et al., "Medical crowdfunding in a healthcare system with universal coverage: an exploratory study," BMC Public Health 2020 (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7653851/).
figure_check: NUANCES/PARTIALLY CONTRADICTS the literal "only one." Medical crowdfunding demonstrably EXISTS in other wealthy nations (Canada, UK, Germany), so "the only [nation]" read as existence is false. BUT the book's actual load-bearing qualifier — "produces the GoFundMe-as-default pattern AT THIS SCALE" — is CONFIRMED as US-distinctive: US median raised is ~3x Canada and ~6x UK, and routine/essential care drives 77.9% of US campaigns vs. ~22–27% elsewhere (in universal-coverage nations crowdfunding fills narrow gaps — experimental/uncovered therapies — not default essential-care financing). The literature explicitly frames European medical crowdfunding as "marginal when compared to the USA." So the source CORRECTS the absolutist phrasing while AFFIRMING the substantive point (US is alone in crowdfunding functioning as a *default* substitute for healthcare financing at population scale).
recommendation: Soften the "only one" and add a cite. Recommended rewrite tracking the PRIOR NOTE's own suggested hedge plus the now-found source: "Among the wealthy nations, no other relies on medical crowdfunding as a default healthcare-financing layer at the U.S. scale: cross-national comparison shows U.S. GoFundMe campaigns raise roughly three times the Canadian and six times the British median, and routine essential-care needs drive nearly four-fifths of U.S. medical campaigns versus roughly a quarter in Canada and the U.K., where crowdfunding fills narrow coverage gaps rather than substituting for the system (Saleh et al. 2020)." Add Saleh et al. 2020 (JAMA Netw Open) to the bibliography — this is the single authoritative comparative source the first pass found missing; it directly supplies the cross-national scale evidence. Keep the "GoFundMe-as-default" framing (well grounded), drop the unqualified "only." Note Ch9 line 202 makes the parallel claim ("None of the comparison countries produces medical crowdfunding at this scale") and should receive the same Saleh et al. 2020 cite for consistency, since the book cross-refers Ch5→Ch9 here.

claim: Method 3 per-ton range '$420 to $13,100/ton, mid ~$2,500' and 'alpha-dominance regime 0.9–1.0' (¶143)
source: INPUTS that ground the computation (all real, externally citable): (1) Dixit, Avinash K., and Robert S. Pindyck, Investment under Uncertainty (Princeton UP, 1994) — the real-options option-value method (V_option) the range is built on; (2) the Arrow–Fisher–Hanemann–Henry quasi-option-value tradition (Arrow & Fisher 1974; Henry 1974) for irreversibility-under-uncertainty, which the α (irreversibility) factor specializes; (3) Hotelling 1931 rent dynamics + Pindyck 1978 JPE stock-dependent cost (the scarcity-multiplier σ anchor). The OUTPUT numbers themselves are computed in-house: book file manuscript/technical-appendix/method3_sensitivity_analysis_2026-04-25.md (formula RCV_M3 = V_option × scarcity_multiplier(σ) × irreversibility_premium(α); McDowell inputs σ≈200–500, α≈0.85–0.95, V_option≈$500–$2,000; irreversibility_premium = 1/(1−α)^β) and TechnicalAppendix_v2.0.0.html lines 4778–4801 which show the endpoint arithmetic explicitly: low $50×1.27×6.67≈$420, high $500×1.31×20≈$13,100, mid ~$2,500. Nearest EXTERNAL comparator for a hostile reader (does NOT publish this output): EPA, Report on the Social Cost of Greenhouse Gases (Dec 2023), central $190/tCO2 at 2% discount; Rennert et al., "Comprehensive evidence implies a higher social cost of CO2," Nature 610 (2022): 687–692, mean $185/tCO2, 5–95% range $44–$413/tCO2.
figure_check: MATCHES the book's own files exactly. The arithmetic reproduces to the dollar: irreversibility_premium 1/(1−0.85)=6.67, 1/(1−0.95)=20; $50×1.27×6.67=$423.5≈$420; $500×1.31×20=$13,100; mid ~$2,500. The α-dominance regime 0.9–1.0 matches method3_sensitivity_analysis §3.1 dominance map (α=0.9–1.0 row) verbatim. EXTERNAL anchors CONTRADICT the ceiling's magnitude as an empirical/market figure: even the most aggressive published SCC (Rennert 95th-pct $413/tCO2) × ~2.07–2.86 tCO2 per short ton coal (EPA/MIT combustion factor) ≈ $855–$1,181/ton-coal from carbon alone — plus EPA non-climate coal health damages (~3.2¢/kWh, NRC Hidden Costs of Energy 2010). That stack lands in the M3 mid-range ($2,500) neighborhood but remains roughly an order of magnitude below the $13,100 ceiling. So the prior note is correct: no public source approaches $13,100/ton; it is solely the high-parameter (α=0.95, β=1) corner of the book's own model.
recommendation: Label as a book-derived estimate showing its inputs — disposition is internal-derivation, NOT unsourceable, and the derivation chain already exists in-repo (method3_sensitivity_analysis_2026-04-25.md + TechAppendix §3.5 lines 4778–4801), so it is fully reconstructable. Two concrete fixes, both cheap: (a) Ch 6 ¶143 already cross-references TechAppendix §3.5 — verify that the live HTML §3.5 anchor surfaces the explicit endpoint arithmetic ($50×1.27×6.67 / $500×1.31×20) so a reviewer can reconstruct the ceiling, not just see the bottom-line range; the arithmetic currently lives in the file but confirm it renders at the cross-referenced anchor. (b) Add one sentence in ¶143 making explicit that $13,100/ton is a model-derived upper bound at the high-parameter (α≈0.95) corner — NOT an empirical market price or an SCC figure — to pre-empt the hostile reader who will reach for EPA SCC ($190/t) and find a ~70× gap. The chapter already says "This is the framework's upper bound" and "reflects parameter uncertainty rather than methodological imprecision," which is most of the way there; the only gap is the absence of an explicit "not an empirical/SCC number" disclaimer and a stated input-set inline. Do NOT cut or soften the figure — it is a legitimate, arithmetic-verified internal derivation grounded in established option-value literature (Dixit-Pindyck 1994; AFHH quasi-option value), and the author's "nothing invented" claim holds here.

claim: Exxon Valdez '$5.5 million of oil spilled (product value)' (¶46)
source: INPUTS that ground the computation: (1) Spill volume ~11 million US gallons = ~257,000-260,000 barrels — multiple federal/authoritative sources concur: US EPA, "Exxon Valdez Spill Profile" (epa.gov/emergency-response/exxon-valdez-spill-profile); NOAA Damage Assessment, Remediation, and Restoration Program, "Exxon Valdez" (darrp.noaa.gov/oil-spills/exxon-valdez); Exxon Valdez Oil Spill Trustee Council Q&A (evostc.state.ak.us/oil-spill-facts/q-and-a/); Britannica "Exxon Valdez oil spill (1989)." (2) 1989 crude price, EIA Petroleum & Other Liquids historical series (eia.gov/dnav/pet): U.S. Crude Oil First Purchase Price annual 1989 = $15.86/bbl (1988 $12.58; 1990 $20.03); Alaska North Slope First Purchase Price annual 1989 = $12.00/bbl, and the spill-month value March 1989 ANS = $11.71/bbl (Jan $9.54, Feb $10.57, Apr $13.36). The cargo was Alaska North Slope crude. The internal archive guidance doc (manuscript/chapters/archive/Chapter__5___GuidanceDoc.md:112) records the basis explicitly: "11 million gallons spilled, market value ~$5.5 million."
figure_check: No external source publishes a "$5.5M product value" for the spilled crude — the only "$5.5 million" tied to Exxon Valdez in sources is lost STATE TOURISM SPENDING (9,400 fewer visitors), an unrelated figure. So the book figure is a book-derived computation, not a citable external value. On the arithmetic the $5.5M figure CORRECTS DOWNWARD if grounded in the price series that actually applied: 260,000 bbl x $11.71/bbl (March 1989 ANS first-purchase, the spilled grade and month) = ~$3.0M; x $12.00 (1989 ANS annual) = ~$3.1M; x $15.86 (1989 U.S. avg first-purchase) = ~$4.1M. The book's $5.5M requires ~$21/bbl, which is only reachable on a WTI-spot basis (WTI spot ran ~$18-21/bbl in early 1989, above first-purchase prices). $5.5M is therefore the HIGH end of plausible and is NOT consistent with the Alaska North Slope first-purchase price of the actual cargo. Critically, the load-bearing rhetorical point survives at any of these inputs: even at the most generous $5.5M the IPG is ~1,270-1,820x; at the ANS-grounded ~$3M it is ~2,300-3,300x (the contrast gets STRONGER, not weaker).
recommendation: Label as a book-derived estimate and show the inputs inline. Recommended: "Exxon Valdez: roughly $3-5.5 million of product value (about 260,000 barrels of Alaska North Slope crude at 1989 prices), against $7-10 billion in cleanup, litigation, and long-run ecological damage — an IPG on the order of 1,000-3,000 times." Cite spill volume to EPA/NOAA/EVOSTC and the price to the EIA crude series. If the author wants to retain a single point figure, $4M (≈260,000 bbl × $15.86 U.S. avg 1989 first-purchase) is better defended than $5.5M; the $5.5M figure should either be dropped to the ANS-grounded ~$3M or explicitly flagged as a WTI-spot-basis upper bound. Either way the 1,200-1,900x IPG band is the conservative/low end and the contrast is rhetorically safe. Do NOT present $5.5M as if any single authority states it — it is the book's own back-of-envelope, and the inputs (not the output) are what get cited.

claim: Bottom-up tier estimates: health $2–$4/ton, environment $1–$3/ton, community costs $5–$15/ton (¶28, ¶30, ¶32, ¶36)
source: INPUTS that ground the construction (all now externally sourced): (1) HEALTH tier — Black Lung Benefits Program $44B distributions through 2009 (book cites GAO/CRS; consistent with GAO black-lung trust-fund series, e.g. GAO-18-351, GAO-19-622T) ÷ cumulative US coal production 1969–2009 = 39,946.7 million short tons (EIA, Monthly/Annual Energy Review Table 7.1 Coal Overview 1949–2011, https://www.eia.gov/totalenergy/data/annual/txt/ptb0701.html) = $1.10/ton, exactly inside the book's stated $1–$1.5/ton black-lung band; peer-reviewed health-cost ceiling: Epstein et al., 'Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal,' Annals NYAS 1219 (2011) 73–98, Table 3 (Appalachian public-health burden $74.6B/yr; combustion air-pollutant health damages $187.5B/yr) and NRC, 'Hidden Costs of Energy' (National Academies, 2010), coal non-climate damages 3.2¢/kWh (~$62B in 2005). (2) ENVIRONMENT tier — Appalachian reclamation/AML shortfall: book's own Ch2 $3.7–$6B figure corroborated by OSMRE AML Fund data ($14.23B collected / $6.57B distributed as of 9/30/2025, https://www.osmre.gov/programs/reclaiming-abandoned-mine-lands) and Appalachian Voices WV inventory gap ($1.21B federal vs $4.5B state, https://appvoices.org/2019/10/11/aml-problems/); Epstein Table 3 AML best/high $8.78B and land-disturbance/methane lines. (3) COMMUNITY tier — Hendryx & Ahern, 'Mortality in Appalachian Coal Mining Regions: The Value of Statistical Life Lost,' Public Health Reports 124 (2009), VSL-of-mortality cost range $16.98B–$84.54B/yr, best $42B (https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/003335490912400411), which is also the basis for Epstein's $74.6B Appalachia line.
figure_check: The aggregate per-ton tier OUTPUTS are not published by any external source and none should be expected — they are the book's own nationally-allocated bottom-up construction. Where checkable, the inputs MATCH and the framing is genuinely conservative: black-lung sub-component computes to $1.10/ton vs book's stated $1–$1.5/ton (MATCHES). The full tiers are far BELOW the comparable peer-reviewed per-ton figures — Epstein 2011 (best $345.3B at 17.84¢/kWh ÷ ~1.05B short tons) implies roughly $71/ton for Appalachian public health alone, ~$8/ton AMLs, ~$179/ton combustion air-pollution health — so the book's $2–4 / $1–3 / $5–15 tiers are an order of magnitude smaller than Epstein's, confirming (not contradicting) the 'conservative' characterization. The difference is methodological: the book allocates a cumulative-stock liability (e.g. $44B trust fund) across cumulative national tonnage, whereas Epstein/Hendryx allocate annual damages across annual tonnage; both are internally valid, and the book's choice is the more conservative denominator.
recommendation: Label explicitly as a book-derived bottom-up estimate and footnote each tier to its underlying public-component inputs so the 'conservative' framing is auditable: HEALTH → Black Lung Benefits Program $44B (GAO/CRS) ÷ EIA Table 7.1 cumulative tonnage, plus the Epstein 2011 / NRC 2010 health-cost literature as the (much larger) external comparator; ENVIRONMENT → Ch2 $3.7–$6B reclamation gap corroborated by OSMRE AML Fund + Appalachian Voices, plus Epstein AML/water lines; COMMUNITY → Hendryx & Ahern (2009) VSL mortality-cost range as the named peer-reviewed anchor. Adding a single footnote stating 'tier outputs are the book's own allocation; external lifecycle studies (Epstein 2011; NRC 2010; Hendryx & Ahern 2009) estimate substantially higher per-ton figures, which is why these tiers are presented as a conservative floor' would make the claim fully defensible. Do NOT cite any single source as supplying the aggregate per-ton tiers — that figure is the book's own. Keep the existing inline black-lung cite; it verifies arithmetically.

claim: Method 2 Norway 'approximately fifty dollars' / '~$48' per barrel-of-oil-equivalent revealed-preference figure (¶141, ¶174)
source: OUTPUT is a book computation (Technical Appendix §11.5 "Norway petroleum calibration," echoed §3.4), not an externally published figure. The INPUTS that ground it are all externally confirmed by primary sources: (1) GPFG value ~$2.0T end-2025 (21,268 billion NOK) — Norges Bank Investment Management (NBIM), Annual Report 2025 / press release "Strong return in 2025" (2026), https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/reports/2025/annual-report-2025/ ; (2) cumulative NCS production ~8.9 billion Sm³ o.e. (~55-56B BOE at 6.29 BOE/Sm³), ~57% of ~15.72 billion Sm³ o.e. total recoverable resources — Norwegian Offshore Directorate / Norwegianpetroleum.no, "Resource accounts for the Norwegian shelf" (as of 31 Dec 2025), https://www.norskpetroleum.no/en/petroleum-resources/resource-accounts/ ; Sm³→BOE factor 6.29 per Norwegian Offshore Directorate conversion table, https://www.sodir.no/en/about-us/use-of-content/conversion-table/ ; (3) state capture rate ~70-80% of petroleum wealth — Norwegian Petroleum Directorate / Ministry of Finance, Norskpetroleum.no "The government's revenues." Adjacent Method 1 inputs also confirmed: EPA emission factor 0.4319 t CO2/barrel at 5.80 mmBtu/barrel (EPA GHG Equivalencies Calculator; EIA CO2 emissions coefficients). Separately, the Ch6 ¶221 narrative carbon-tax anchor is confirmed: petroleum-sector CO2 tax NOK 761/t (2023) and NOK 543/t (2021) — energifaktanorge.no "Taxes and emissions trading"; Norden TemaNord 2023:520 §Norway. NOTE: the carbon tax is NOT an input to the per-BOE Method 2 computation (which uses fund value ÷ capture rate ÷ cumulative BOE), contrary to the prior note's framing.
figure_check: Inputs MATCH primary sources exactly (GPFG $2.0T = NBIM 21,268B NOK; 8.9B Sm³ o.e. = Offshore Directorate; 57% produced = Norskpetroleum; 0.43 t CO2/bbl = EPA 0.4319). The OUTPUT is internal and unpublished externally, as expected for a revealed-preference per-BOE estimate. CONFIRMED the internal $48-vs-$50 inconsistency the deep-pass §8 flagged: Technical Appendix §11.5 (line 4241-4245) computes $2.0T/0.75=$2.67T ÷ 55B BOE ≈ $48/BOE and explicitly states this is "down marginally from prior $50/BOE anchor at $1.9T fund value / 50B BOE; updated production denominator drives a small directional shift." So §11.5 was updated to $48 (fund $1.9T→$2.0T, denominator 50B→55B BOE), and Ch6 ¶174 correctly uses $48, but Ch6 ¶141 ("approximately fifty dollars") and §3.4 (line 861: "$50/BOE Method 2 anchor") were left at the superseded $50 value — a sync miss, not a real disagreement.
recommendation: (1) DISPOSITION: keep as book-derived estimate; the figure is legitimate internal derivation with fully-sourced inputs — do NOT cut or soften the substance. (2) RESOLVE the $48-vs-$50 inconsistency by standardizing on $48/BOE (the current §11.5 value reflecting $2.0T fund / 55B BOE), since ¶174's convergence table already uses $48 and the table is the precision context. Specifically: change Ch6 ¶141 "a documented per-barrel-of-oil-equivalent figure of approximately fifty dollars" to "approximately forty-eight dollars" (or "approximately fifty dollars" can stay ONLY if treated as a deliberate round number AND §3.4 line 861 + the §11.5 derivation are all reconciled to one rounding convention — but cleaner to use $48 throughout). Also update Technical Appendix §3.4 line 861 ("$50/BOE Method 2 anchor") and the §3.4-to-§11.5 cross-reference to $48 to match §11.5. (3) The §11.5 derivation already shows its inputs and arithmetic with sources — this is the model; ensure the §3.4 main-text Method 2 box cites §11.5's input chain (GPFG $2.0T ÷ 0.75 capture ÷ 55B BOE) explicitly and labels the result a book-derived revealed-preference estimate. (4) CORRECT the prior note: cite the carbon tax (NOK 761/t 2023) as the Ch6 ¶221 NARRATIVE Norway-pricing anchor, NOT as an input to the Method 2 per-BOE figure; the per-BOE figure's inputs are GPFG value, capture rate, and cumulative BOE, all sourced above.

claim: Kimberley Process empirical claim (line 221): the 'conflict-free' diamond ethical premium 'stays with the retailer and the certification infrastructure; it does not reach the miners.'
source: PEER-REVIEWED ANCHOR FOR THE GENERALIZED INCIDENCE CLAIM (strongest, directly on point for the coffee variant the same sentence invokes): Karol Jan Borowiecki & Tobias Naegele (Naegele), "Where does the Fair Trade money go? How much consumers pay extra for Fair Trade coffee and how this value is split along the value chain," World Development, vol. 133, art. 104998 (2020). Finding: consumers pay roughly $1.50/lb extra (range $1.10–$2.03); the FARMER receives about ONE-SIXTH of the consumer premium; MOST of the premium accrues to the roaster's profit margin. (Working-paper version: DIW Berlin Discussion Paper No. 1783.) — DIAMOND STRUCTURAL INPUTS: (1) Global Witness, "Return of the Blood Diamond" (2017) and Global Witness's December 2011 withdrawal from the Kimberley Process ("Global Witness leaves the Kimberley Process") — KP certifies "conflict-free" without addressing wages/conditions; little evidence diamond wealth reaches ordinary people. (2) Wilson Center, "The Misattribution of Africa's Natural Resource Wealth: An Examination of the Diamond Industry" — ~1 million African artisanal miners earn <$1/day against an ~$81.4B/yr industry; value captured between miners and trading hubs (e.g., $22.8B rough through the UAE in 2021). (3) Diamond Development Initiative / Global Witness estimates: ~1 million African alluvial diggers earning <$1–2/day. (4) Value-chain margin structure (Bain & Company Global Diamond Industry Report; Statista "Operating margins in the diamond value chain"): rough-production margins ~16–24%, midstream cutting/polishing ~1–8%, retail ~11–14% — consistent with downstream (retail/brand) margin capture rather than upstream/labor capture.
figure_check: MATCHES the structural direction of the book's claim but does NOT directly confirm the specific diamond premium-incidence mechanism. No source isolates a measured "conflict-free diamond ethical premium" and traces what share reaches miners. The closest measured analogue is the FAIR-TRADE COFFEE case (Naegele 2020), which DIRECTLY MATCHES the book's worded generalization: farmer/producer captures ~1/6, most stays with the roaster (the brand/intermediary) — i.e., the premium largely does not reach the producer. For diamonds specifically, the supporting evidence is structural (artisanal miners <$1–2/day; KP ignores wages; retail/brand carries the larger consumer-facing margin), which is consistent with but not a direct measurement of the premium's incidence.
recommendation: RETAIN the claim but attach citations and soften the diamond sentence from a flat empirical assertion to a structurally-supported one. Concretely: (a) For the diamond sentence, cite Global Witness ("Return of the Blood Diamond," 2017; and its 2011 KP withdrawal) + Wilson Center / DDI for the <$1–2/day artisanal-miner figure and the KP's exclusion of wages/conditions; this fully supports "the KP certifies conflict-free without reaching the miners whose conditions the cost was born in." The narrower mechanistic phrase "the premium stays with the retailer and the certification infrastructure" is an inference from value-chain margin structure (Bain/Statista) rather than a directly measured incidence — either footnote it as such or rephrase to "there is no evidence the premium reaches the miners; artisanal diggers continue to earn under $2/day." (b) For the fair-trade-coffee generalization, ADD the drop-in cite Naegele (2020), World Development 133:104998 — it is dispositive and directly worded ("farmer receives ~1/6 of the consumer premium; most goes to the roaster"). (c) The carbon-offset and ESG-screen parallels each still need their own source if retained (e.g., for offsets: The Guardian/Die Zeit/SourceMaterial 2023 Verra rainforest-credit investigation; for ESG: an academic ESG-flows study) — flag both as currently unsourced. Net: this is an analytical "immune-response" argument in a thought-experiment chapter; it is defensible with the above citations and a one-notch softening of the diamond mechanism phrasing, not a fabrication.

claim: Direct health cost 'between two and four dollars per ton at the national level' (line 38), including Medicare/Medicaid shift, uncompensated care, bankrupt-employer private bills; only the $1-1.50 Black Lung subcomponent is program-anchored.
source: INPUTS that ground the computation: (1) BLACK LUNG FLOOR (program-anchored): CRS Report R45261, "The Black Lung Program, the Black Lung Disability Trust Fund, and the Excise Tax on Coal" (Congressional Research Service) — coal excise tax $1.10/ton underground / $0.55/ton surface, capped at 4.4% of sales price; FY2021 ~$189M collected (96.2% at the $1.10 rate); IRA 2022 made rates permanent. https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R45261 . (2) AGGREGATE HEALTH BURDEN the per-ton sits inside: Epstein, P.R., Buonocore, J.J., Eckerle, K., Hendryx, M., et al. (2011), "Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal," Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences 1219(1):73-98, doi:10.1111/j.1749-6632.2010.05890.x — read directly from PDF: "Public Health in Appalachia Related to Mining" = $74.6 billion/yr (4.36 cents/kWh; 10,923 excess deaths/yr x VSL $7.5M, 91% coal-to-electricity adjustment, p.83); total coal externalities "a third to over one-half of a trillion dollars annually" (p.73, abstract); carcinogen emissions $11B/yr (0.66 cents/kWh, excluded from total to avoid double-counting, p.82); rail-transport fatalities $1.8B (0.09 cents/kWh, p.84). (3) NRC 2009, "Hidden Costs of Energy" (National Research Council) — coal damages $62B/yr (3.2 cents/kWh), 52% health-related (PMC3060022). (4) DENOMINATOR: EIA Annual Energy Review Table 7.1 — US coal production 2005=1,131.5M short tons, 2008=1,171.8M, 2010=1,084.4M. https://www.eia.gov/totalenergy/data/annual/txt/ptb0701.html
figure_check: CONFIRMS the prior note. No external source publishes a clean $/short-ton narrow-direct-medical-spending split, so the $2-4/ton OUTPUT cannot be matched/corrected/contradicted directly. What CAN be checked: the Black Lung subcomponent ($1-1.50/ton) is independently program-anchored via CRS R45261's $1.10/ton excise and reconciles. The full Epstein health aggregate, naively allocated ($74.6B Appalachian-mining burden / ~1.13B short tons 2005 = ~$66/ton), is far ABOVE $2-4/ton — but that aggregate is VSL-monetized excess MORTALITY, not the narrow direct medical-spending (Medicare/Medicaid shift + uncompensated care + private bills) the book scopes. The book's $2-4/ton is thus a deliberately conservative narrow-scope figure roughly an order of magnitude below the full-burden allocation, internally consistent with the chapter's stated "conservative floor" posture. The $2-4 range itself is the author's allocation, not a published number.
recommendation: Label explicitly as a book-derived per-ton estimate showing its inputs. Recommended pre-pub-queue framing: keep the $1-1.50 Black Lung subcomponent as program-anchored (cite CRS R45261 / Ch2 Trust Fund treatment); present the remaining $0.50-2.50 (Medicare/Medicaid shift + uncompensated care + bankrupt-employer private bills, bringing the total to $2-4) as an explicitly author-allocated conservative estimate anchored to the aggregate coal public-health literature (Epstein et al. 2011 $74.6B Appalachian-mining burden + NRC 2009 $62B coal damages, 52% health), with a sentence noting these aggregates monetize the FULL burden (mortality via VSL) at an order of magnitude above the narrow direct-medical-spend figure the chapter prices, so $2-4/ton is conservative by construction. Do NOT cite Epstein/NRC as if they publish a $2-4/ton direct-health figure — they do not; cite them as the aggregate ground that bounds the author's conservative allocation. Carry the "internally-estimated, anchored-to-Epstein/NRC, Black-Lung-subcomponent-program-anchored" flag forward to the external occupational-health reviewer already named in the pre-pub queue.

claim: Environmental degradation cost allocated at '$1-3 per ton' (line 52).
source: INPUTS that ground the per-ton allocation (all externally sourced): (1) Epstein PR, Buonocore JJ, Eckerle K, Hendryx M, et al. "Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal." Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences 1219 (2011): 73-98 — Table 3/Table 4 monetized US coal externalities, 2008 USD; environmental-degradation categories (land disturbance, carcinogens mostly to water from waste, abandoned-mine-lands per ELI 2007, and mercury productivity/mental-retardation/cardiovascular). (2) Appalachian Voices / Reclaiming Appalachia Coalition, "Repairing the Damage: The Costs of Delaying Reclamation at Modern-Era Mines" (2021) — $3.8B available bonds vs $7.5-9.8B reclamation need across seven Appalachian states to reclaim ~633,000 acres (grounds the chapter's line 48 inputs). (3) Hendryx M & Luo (WVU), "Self-Reported Cancer Rates in Two Rural Areas of West Virginia with and without Mountaintop Coal Mining," Journal of Community Health (2012) — ~60,000 additional cancer cases among ~1.2M MTR-area residents. (4) National Research Council, "Hidden Costs of Energy: Unpriced Consequences of Energy Production and Use" (2010) — coal non-climate damages ~3.2 cents/kWh / ~$62B (2005). Context-only (different geography): Yale Environment Review / Colombia coal study, mining-waste-to-soil $39.78-59.61/ton.
figure_check: CORRECTS-toward-conservative (the book figure is internally consistent and conservatively low, not contradicted). No source publishes a $1-3/short-ton environmental-degradation allocation as such. Converting Epstein 2011 best-estimate environmental-degradation categories (land disturbance + carcinogens-to-water + AML + mercury, ~$10.9B/yr, 2008 USD) against ~1.17B short tons of 2008 US coal production yields ~$9.3/ton; using Epstein's LOW-end values yields ~$3.7/ton. The book's $1-3/ton therefore sits BELOW even Epstein's low end — fully consistent with the chapter's explicit "use the lower figure / conservative floor, not a ceiling" discipline (line 28). The underlying damages are all real and sourced (AMD perpetual treatment; $3.8B-vs-$7.5-9.8B reclamation bond gap; 60,000 MTR cancer cases; mercury 48 tons/yr from US coal combustion per Epstein). The OUTPUT per-ton division is the book's own apportionment; the INPUTS match published figures.
recommendation: Label explicitly as a book-derived conservative estimate showing its inputs. Concrete fix: add an endnote anchoring the aggregate to Epstein et al. 2011 (cite the per-kWh Table 4 figures and note that allocating Epstein's environmental-degradation categories against US coal tonnage yields ~$3.7-9.3/ton, so the chapter's $1-3/ton is a deliberately conservative subset/floor), with the AML bond gap cited to Appalachian Voices 2021 and the cancer burden to Hendryx & Luo 2012. The PRIOR NOTE's recommendation is correct and confirmed by the Epstein conversion: keep the $1-3/ton, disclose the per-ton division as the book's own apportionment in the pre-pub queue, and do NOT present it as an externally published per-ton figure. No softening or cutting needed — the figure is defensible and conservative.

claim: Community-disruption cost: 'in the range of five to fifteen dollars per ton' (line 62); deep-pass §9 calls this the least-sourced component figure and second-largest non-carbon head ($5).
source: No external source publishes a $5-15/short-ton "sustained regional decline" allocation as an output. The legitimate INPUT sources that can ground a transparent book-derived estimate are: (1) Epstein, P.R., Buonocore, J.J., Eckerle, K., Hendryx, M., et al. (2011), "Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal," Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences 1219(1):73-98, doi:10.1111/j.1749-6632.2010.05890.x — monetizes the Appalachian community public-health burden at $74.6 billion/yr (4.36 cents/kWh) and explicitly contrasts it with the $8.08 billion (2005 US$) in direct+indirect+induced economic benefits of Appalachian coal mining; this is the closest published full-cost-accounting itemization, though it is denominated per-kWh and is a public-health/mortality head, not a discrete "community disruption / regional decline" head. (2) Hendryx, M. & Ahern, M.M. (2009), "Mortality in Appalachian coal mining regions: the value of statistical life lost," Public Health Reports 124(4):541-550 — net-cost framing ($42B premature-death cost, range $16.98B-$84.54B, vs $8B benefits). (3) Shrinking-community fixed-cost-overcapacity / stranded-infrastructure literature (e.g., Yale Law Journal, "The New Minimal Cities," 2019; municipal-finance work on per-capita service-cost escalation under population loss) — grounds the infrastructure-overcapacity and multiplier-loss mechanisms the book invokes at line 60 but produces no per-ton coal allocation. (4) Boone County WV severance-collapse case (employment -85%, severance revenue -87%, population -15% 2008-2018) as a documented regional-decline anchor. McDowell cumulative tonnage inputs needed to perform the allocation: peak ~29M tons (1942), ~90M tons/yr regional peak claims, multi-decade production history (WVGES; ARC; NPS Coal Heritage Survey).
figure_check: Neither MATCHES nor CONTRADICTS — no external figure of this construction exists to compare against. The book's $5-15/ton is its own allocation. Sanity check on plausibility: Epstein's $74.6B/yr Appalachian community burden, if allocated against roughly ~1 billion short tons of annual US coal production in that era, implies on the order of tens of dollars per ton for the Appalachian-attributable share — so a $5-15/ton community-disruption sub-figure is order-of-magnitude defensible as a conservative carve-out, but only as a book-constructed estimate, not as a number any cited economist "produces." The prior pass's diagnosis is correct: the claim is presented as externally sourced ("the figures they produce") when it is in fact the book's own derivation, and the book does not even show its derivation inputs.
recommendation: Relabel explicitly as a book-derived estimate and show the inputs. Drop the "the figures they produce" phrasing (which implies a specific external per-ton figure exists). Replace with language that (a) cites the real input literature — Epstein et al. 2011 (Appalachian community burden $74.6B/yr vs $8.08B benefits) and Hendryx & Ahern 2009 (net mortality cost) and the shrinking-community fixed-cost/stranded-infrastructure literature — and (b) states plainly that the book carves a conservative $5/ton community-disruption sub-figure out of that documented gap, distinct from the health head already counted, then uses the low end. Suggested form: "Full-cost-accounting work on Appalachian coal (Epstein et al., Annals NYAS 2011) monetizes the community-level burden at tens of billions of dollars a year against roughly $8 billion in regional economic benefit; the shrinking-community fixed-cost literature documents why per-capita service costs rise as population collapses. Carving a conservative community-disruption allocation out of that gap — separate from the health costs already counted — puts it in the range of five to fifteen dollars per ton. I'll use the low end. Five dollars per ton." STRONG pre-pub-queue flag retained: this is the least-sourced component and the second-largest non-carbon head, so the relabel-and-show-inputs fix is not optional. Also reconcile the parallel claim in Ch 6 line 36/38 ("A conservative community-cost estimate comes in between $5 and $15 per ton") which carries the same number and the same sourcing weakness; the 2026-05-11 cross-chapter audit (research/audits/cross-chapter-consistency-audit_2026-05-11.md, item X-2) already established the figure originates in the book, not in Ch 2's qualitative enumeration.

claim: Lineage-labor cost: 'development economists... regional estimates in the single-digit billions... per-ton $1-5' (line 98).
source: INPUTS that ground the derivation (all real, citable): (1) Chetty, R. & Hendren, N. (2018), "The Impacts of Neighborhoods on Intergenerational Mobility II: County-Level Estimates," Quarterly Journal of Economics 133(3):1163-1228 — publishes causal county place-effects on adult earnings for low-income (25th-pctile) children, per-year-of-exposure percentage effects; and Chetty, Friedman, Hendren, Jones & Porter (2018/2022), "The Opportunity Atlas," NBER WP 25147 / QJE — per-child lifetime-earnings effects of place (e.g. moving to a 75th-pctile tract raises a low-income child's lifetime earnings by ~$387,000). (2) McDowell County scale: U.S. Census (peak ~100K residents 1950, <20K by 2020; 2022 median household income $28,235; poverty 37.6%) — already locked in the Ch 8 Pass-1 fact-check artifact. (3) Cumulative McDowell coal tonnage on the order of ~1 billion tons (peak ~29M tons/yr 1942 per WV Geological Survey / Coal Heritage), the denominator for the per-ton allocation. NO source publishes the OUTPUT (a per-ton lineage-labor figure or a county-aggregate mobility-loss total). The leading full-cost-accounting study — Epstein et al. (2011), "Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal," Annals NYAS 1219:73-98 (best estimate $345.3B/yr US externalities, itemized: air pollutants, GHG, land/property/public-health) — contains NO intergenerational-mobility/lineage-labor component, confirming this is a book-original cost category surfaced by the Commons Inversion Test (line 88).
figure_check: No source MATCHES, CORRECTS, or CONTRADICTS the per-ton figure because none exists — the output is unpublished anywhere. The derivation is internally COHERENT and reconstructible from the named inputs: thousands of affected low-income children across the extraction generations x ~$100K-$400K per-child mobility loss (Opportunity Insights range) approximates low single-digit billions for a county of McDowell's scale; divided by ~1B+ cumulative tons yields roughly $1-5/ton, bracketing the $2 placeholder. The "single-digit billions" and "$1-5/ton" are therefore the book's own order-of-magnitude estimate, not an external figure. SEPARATE CONSISTENCY FLAG (out of scope but worth surfacing): one search return reported McDowell as "about average for income mobility, better than ~46% of counties," which would tension with line 92's "floor of national outcomes" framing — the Opportunity Atlas county-rank should be re-verified for the line-92 claim independently; it does not affect the line-98 lineage-labor disposition.
recommendation: Label as a book-derived estimate showing its inputs. The prose already concedes imprecision ("though imprecise," "conservative placeholder," "the reader can decide"), which is the right posture — but the phrase "Development economists... have produced regional estimates in the single-digit billions" reads as an external attribution that does not exist as stated. Two fixes, either acceptable: (A) RE-ATTRIBUTE to the actual inputs: e.g., "Place-effect estimates from the Opportunity Insights project (Chetty and Hendren) put the per-child lifetime cost of growing up in a floor-mobility county in the hundreds of thousands of dollars; applied across the affected children of a county of McDowell's scale, the aggregate runs into the single-digit billions. Spread across the roughly one billion tons McDowell produced, that is on the order of one to five dollars per ton — the book's own allocation, not a figure any economist has published per ton." (B) SOFTEN the attribution: change "Development economists... have produced regional estimates" to "Applying development-economics place-effect findings yields regional estimates," making clear the aggregation is the book's. Add to the Stage 5 pre-pub review queue as a book-derived estimate (consistent with the existing prior-note concession). Cite Chetty-Hendren 2018 QJE + Opportunity Atlas as the input ground; cite Epstein et al. 2011 as the precedent for itemized coal full-cost accounting while noting lineage labor is a book-original component the literature omits.

claim: John L. Lewis / UMW 'declined to press' black-lung compensation 'reportedly on the grounds that raising it would interfere with mechanization' (line 120).
source: Primary scholarly anchors (both already named in the prior note, both confirmed real and on-point): (1) Alan Derickson, "The United Mine Workers of America and the Recognition of Occupational Respiratory Diseases, 1902-1968," American Journal of Public Health 81(6), 1991, pp. 782-790 (peer-reviewed; PMC1405142) — the dedicated scholarly treatment of UMWA conduct on miners' respiratory disease through 1968; (2) Alan Derickson, Black Lung: Anatomy of a Public Health Disaster (Cornell University Press, 1998); (3) Barbara Ellen Smith, Digging Our Own Graves: Coal Miners and the Struggle over Black Lung Disease (Temple UP, 1987; repr. Haymarket 2020). Direct firsthand-staff corroboration of the motivation framing: Barbara Berney (health analyst, UMW Welfare and Retirement Fund staff to 1978), "The Rise and Fall of the UMW Fund," Facing South / Southern Exposure — states the Fund's occupational-health work "was limited ... by Lewis' commitment to labor peace and industry mechanization." The "decided not to raise the black lung issue because it might impede mechanization / required higher coal output" formulation also appears in the secondary public-health literature summary (Disabled World, "Black Lung Disease").
figure_check: MATCHES (substantively, with one calibration).
