Multiple independent sources confirm the two load-bearing components: (a) the UMWA under Lewis traded acceptance of mechanization for the Welfare and Retirement Fund and prioritized output/wages over occupational-health agitation, and (b) the union under Lewis did not press the black-lung issue, with mechanization/labor-peace named as the reason — Berney attributes the Fund's limited black-lung work directly to "Lewis' commitment to labor peace and industry mechanization." Calibration: the strongest sources frame this as Lewis prioritizing mechanization-plus-Fund and therefore NOT agitating on black lung, rather than as documented explicit internal statements that he refused specifically to protect mechanization. The book's "reportedly ... on the grounds that" hedge correctly captures this evidentiary posture; sources support the substance without a single smoking-gun internal quotation.
recommendation: Drop-in cite and retain. The "reportedly" hedge is appropriate and well-calibrated to the source base. Attach a footnote sourcing the claim to Derickson 1991 (AJPH 81:782-790) and/or Derickson 1998 (Black Lung, Cornell UP) plus Smith 1987 (Digging Our Own Graves); Berney's Facing South piece can serve as a corroborating firsthand-staff reference for the mechanization-priority motivation specifically. No softening or cutting needed — this is not an invented motivation attribution; it is a standard reading in the black-lung historiography. Optional precision tightening: if the author wants to remove residual risk, phrase as "prioritized mechanization and the Welfare and Retirement Fund over pressing the black-lung issue," which is what the sources most directly establish.

claim: Across-market rise in cost-burdened renter share (line 190), and the causal claim that 'asset-class consolidation is producing it, not supply restriction' (line 192).
source: DESCRIPTIVE part (sourced-now): Joint Center for Housing Studies of Harvard University, The State of the Nation's Housing 2024 — 50% of renters (22.4M households) cost-burdened in 2022, all-time record, +3.2pp vs 2019, +9pp vs 2001; cost burden rose across income strata including middle-income ($45k-75k +5.4pp to 41%). https://www.jchs.harvard.edu/state-nations-housing-2024 and https://www.jchs.harvard.edu/arh-2024-cost-burden-share . SUPPORTING CONTEXT for the consolidation mechanism (real, but does not establish the causal-attribution claim): (1) U.S. DOJ v. RealPage antitrust action — investigation 2022, complaint Aug 2024 (six-landlord follow-on Jan 2025), proposed settlement Nov 24 / filed Dec 1 2025 — Sherman Act §§1-2 algorithmic price coordination (timeline in chapter is ACCURATE). (2) Gurun, Wu, Xiao & Xiao, "Do Wall Street Landlords Undermine Renters' Welfare?," Review of Financial Studies 2023 — institutional acquisition raises rents ~60% above market increase, spillover to non-investor landlords. (3) GAO-24-106643 Rental Housing: Institutional Investment in Single-Family Homes (2024); Philadelphia Fed WP 24-13 (2024). CONTRARY/competing causal literature the chapter must reckon with: Gyourko et al. "Why is the rent so darn high? The role of growing demand to live in housing-supply-inelastic cities" (J. Urban Economics 2021) attributes 17-73% (preferred >50%) of rent increase to LOCATION DEMAND; NBER w22816 (Albouy/Ehrlich/Liu) and 2026 follow-on work attribute most of the affordability decline to INCOME INEQUALITY at the top, not supply or consolidation; Glaeser & Gyourko (Brookings BPEA Spring 2025) maintain the supply-constraint reading.
figure_check: DESCRIPTIVE claim MATCHES the source: JCHS 2024 documents ~50% record cost-burden share rising across income strata and (per its metro-level reporting) across Sun Belt supply-expanded markets (Atlanta among fastest-rising) as well as coastal supply-restricted ones — the "across markets with widely varying supply elasticities" framing is supported. CAUSAL claim is NOT MATCHED/CONTRADICTED by any single source: no authoritative source adjudicates that asset-class consolidation rather than supply restriction produces the across-market rise. The peer-reviewed decomposition literature points the OTHER way on primacy — Gyourko 2021 (demand to live in inelastic cities), NBER w22816 (top-income inequality), Glaeser/Gyourko 2025 (supply) — and reviews explicitly state the financialization↔rent-burden link "has not yet been explored both theoretically and empirically" and that the field has "no consensus" on which driver is primary. The chapter's own line 186 correctly cites the Glaeser/Gyourko/Schuetz supply-side literature it then argues against. So the causal sentence is the BOOK'S ARGUMENT within a contested debate, not a sourced finding — confirming the prior-pass flag.
recommendation: Keep the descriptive claim as-is with a JCHS 2024 page-cite (State of the Nation's Housing 2024, cost-burden section; the companion America's Rental Housing 2024 carries the metro-level supply-elasticity breakdown if a per-metro cite is wanted). For the causal sentence (line 192), do NOT present "what is producing it is the framework's subject / asset-class consolidation ... not supply restriction" as a settled finding. The chapter already does most of the right thing in lines 188-194 by framing both mechanisms as "real" and "operating simultaneously" — tighten line 190's closing assertion ("supply restriction is not it") and line 192's "What is producing it is..." to explicitly mark them as the book's interpretive claim, e.g. "The framework reads this residual as the signature of a second mechanism — asset-class consolidation — operating on top of local supply elasticity," and add a footnote acknowledging the contested causal literature (Gyourko 2021 demand-in-inelastic-cities; Albouy et al. NBER w22816 income-inequality; Glaeser/Gyourko 2025 supply) so the causal attribution is positioned as a contribution to the debate, not a finding the cited descriptive source supports. Cite the DOJ RealPage docket and Gurun et al. 2023 as support for the EXISTENCE/plausibility of the consolidation mechanism, not as proof it is the across-market driver. Net: label as a book-derived interpretive argument with real supporting evidence; remove any phrasing implying JCHS or the empirical literature establishes consolidation-over-supply causation.

claim: $10-15T figure is 'larger than every government subsidy program combined... all international development assistance... all charitable giving... combined' and 'the largest continuous wealth transfer in human history' (line 220).
source: The SUPERLATIVE itself is the book's own rhetorical/derived output (no external source publishes it). But the three COMPARISON DENOMINATORS are each externally sourceable: (1) Government subsidies — World Bank, "Detox Development: Repurposing Environmentally Harmful Subsidies" (2023): global DIRECT government expenditure across fossil/agriculture/fisheries ≈ $1.25T/yr; explicit+implicit across those sectors ≈ $7T/yr (https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2023/06/15/trillions-wasted-on-subsidies-could-help-address-climate-change). OECD: agricultural support ≈ $842B/yr 2021-23 (https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/subsidies-and-government-support.html). Earth Track / Business for Nature, environmentally harmful subsidies ≈ $2.6T/yr (https://www.earthtrack.net/blog/environmentally-harmful-subsidies-update-26-trillionyear-and-continuing-threat-nature). (2) International development assistance — OECD DAC, ODA = USD 223.3B in 2023 (https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/policy-issues/official-development-assistance-oda.html). (3) Charitable giving — Citi GPS, "Philanthropy and the Global Economy": total global giving ≈ $2.3T incl. time, ≈ $1.3T monetary individual giving/yr (https://www.privatebank.citibank.com/newcpb-media/media/documents/insights/Philanthropy-and-global-economy.pdf); Giving USA 2024: U.S. charitable giving = $557.16B in 2023 (https://givingusa.org/giving-usa-u-s-charitable-giving-totaled-557-16-billion-in-2023/).
figure_check: CORROBORATES the comparison directionally, with one caveat. (1) Subsidies: clean/direct global government subsidy spend ≈ $1.25T/yr and even the broadest environmentally-harmful figure is $2.6T/yr — both well below $10-15T, so the comparison HOLDS on an explicit/direct reading. CAVEAT: the single largest subsidy figure ($7T, World Bank/IMF) is ~$5T IMPLICIT undercharging for environmental/climate costs, which conceptually OVERLAPS the book's own "severed cost" — comparing $10-15T severed cost to a $7T figure that is itself mostly severed cost is double-counting the same concept, so the book must compare against the DIRECT/explicit subsidy denominator (~$1.25-2.6T), not the $7T figure. (2) ODA: $223B/yr — $10-15T exceeds it ~45-67x; comparison clearly TRUE. (3) Charitable giving: ~$1.3-2.3T/yr globally — $10-15T exceeds it ~5-11x; comparison HOLDS. Net: all three comparisons are externally verifiable as directionally correct CONDITIONAL on the $10-15T extrapolation (lines 216-218) holding; the superlative rests entirely on that upstream gestured figure.
recommendation: KEEP with two precise fixes; do NOT cut. (1) The chapter already self-discloses the $10-15T as "a gestured claim" with "one sentence of supporting arithmetic" (line 222) and explicitly declines to compress it — this honesty is the right posture and largely defuses the prior-note concern; lean on it. (2) Ground each comparison denominator with a footnote so the superlative is anchored to real numbers rather than gestured: OECD DAC ODA ($223B, 2023); Citi GPS / Giving USA for global charitable giving (~$1.3-2.3T); World Bank Detox Development for direct global subsidy spend (~$1.25T). (3) CRITICAL precision fix on the subsidy comparison: specify "direct government subsidy spending" (not "every government subsidy program"), because the headline $7T global-subsidy figure (IMF/World Bank) is ~70% IMPLICIT environmental undercharging — the same severed-cost concept the book is measuring — so comparing the book's $10-15T against it would double-count. claim: Mian & Sufi: bottom-40% welfare benefit by a factor of 3–4 relative to top-quintile households from debt restructuring vs bank rescue (line 122).
source: Atif Mian, Kamalesh Rao & Amir Sufi, "Household Balance Sheets, Consumption, and the Economic Slump," Quarterly Journal of Economics 128(4):1687-1726 (2013) — full text at https://fairmodel.econ.yale.edu/ec439/sufi2.pdf. Key passages (extracted): "the MPC for households living in ZIP codes with an average annual income of less than $35,000 is three times as large as the MPC for households living in ZIP codes with more than $200,000 in average income"; "ZIP codes that entered the Great Recession with a housing loan-to-value (LTV) ratio of 90% had an MPC out of housing wealth that was three times as large as the MPC of households in ZIP codes with only a 30% housing LTV ratio"; average MPC out of housing wealth = 5-7 cents/dollar; consumption-to-housing-net-worth elasticity 0.6-0.8. Plus Atif Mian & Amir Sufi, House of Debt (Univ. of Chicago Press, 2014): debt-to-asset ratio of bottom 20% = ~80% vs top 20% = ~7% (ch. "Levered Losses"); shared-responsibility-mortgage counterfactual = housing-wealth decline ~$2.5 trillion smaller.
figure_check: CORRECTS / PARTIALLY CONTRADICTS. The source supports a factor of THREE (3x), not "three to four (3-4)," and that factor is stated TWICE in Mian-Rao-Sufi 2013: (1) MPC of <$35k-income ZIPs ≈ 3x MPC of >$200k-income ZIPs; (2) MPC of 90%-LTV ZIPs ≈ 3x MPC of 30%-LTV ZIPs. The "to four" upper bound is not in the source. The source's distributional unit is income/leverage DECILES and the top/bottom 20% (e.g., bottom decile lost 45% of net worth, top decile ~0%; bottom-20% debt-to-asset 80% vs top-20% 7%) — NOT "bottom 40% vs top quintile," which is the book's own reframing. And the source quantity is an MPC (consumption-response) differential, not a published "welfare benefit from restructuring-vs-bank-rescue" ratio; House of Debt does not state a single bottom-40%-vs-top-quintile welfare statistic. So the book has correctly captured the spirit (poor/levered households' consumption is ~3x more sensitive, hence restructuring delivers disproportionately to them) but the specific number ("3-4"), the population brackets ("bottom 40% / top quintile"), and the metric ("welfare benefit") are looser than what the source actually says.
recommendation: Do NOT cut — the claim is real and grounded; it is mis-specified, not invented. Two clean fixes, in order of preference: (1) PRECISION-CORRECT to the source: change "by a factor of three to four relative to top-quintile households" to "with a marginal propensity to consume roughly three times that of high-income/low-leverage households" and cite Mian, Rao & Sufi, QJE 2013 (the 3x MPC differential for <$35k vs >$200k income ZIPs and for 90% vs 30% LTV) plus House of Debt 2014 for the levered-losses distribution (bottom-20% debt-to-asset 80% vs top-20% 7%). This converts a fuzzy "welfare benefit by 3-4x" into the exact, defensible mechanism. (2) If the author prefers to keep the welfare/distributional framing rather than the MPC framing, RELABEL it as the book's own characterization ("Mian and Sufi's evidence implies the bottom of the distribution would have benefited several-fold more...") and drop the false-precision "three to four" / "top-quintile" specifics, since those exact brackets are not in the source. Either way, add a direct page-cite to House of Debt's "Levered Losses" chapter and the QJE 2013 article. AVOID leaving "three to four" + "bottom forty percent" + "top-quintile" as written, since each of those three specifics diverges from the underlying source and is the kind of false precision an empirically literate reader (the claim's own invited "defend or rebut on empirical grounds") can rebut.

claim: Ly & Soman 2020 and Kenworthy & Igra 2024 confirm crowdfunding scale + differential success rates (line 202).
source: CORRECTED/CONFIRMED cite for the "Kenworthy and Igra" element: Mark Igra & Nora Kenworthy, et al., "Crowdfunding as a formal financing source... Medical Crowdfunding and Disparities in Health Care Access in the United States, 2016-2020," American Journal of Public Health 112(3):491-498 (2022) — 437,000+ GoFundMe campaigns; raised >$2B from 21.7M donations; only 12% met goals (16% received nothing); success lowest where medical debt highest and insurance coverage lowest = differential success mirroring healthcare-access disparities. PLUS the 2024 monograph: Nora Kenworthy, Crowded Out: The True Costs of Crowdfunding Healthcare (Cambridge, MA: MIT Press, 2024), ISBN 9780262548038. Same-sentence anchor (adjacent, not the assigned claim): Lauren Berliner & Nora Kenworthy, "Producing a worthy illness: Personal crowdfunding amidst financial crisis," Social Science & Medicine 187:233-242 (2017). NOT located: any "Ly and Soman 2020" medical-crowdfunding paper. Kim Ly & Dilip Soman (Rotman/BEAR) are real co-authors but their joint work is on nudging (e.g., Ly, Mazar, Zhao & Soman, "A Practitioner's Guide to Nudging," 2013), not a 2020 crowdfunding study confirming scale/differential success.
figure_check: PARTIALLY CONFIRMS, PARTIALLY CORRECTS. The claimed pattern (scale + differential success mirroring healthcare-access disparities) is robustly confirmed by the source literature — but by Igra & Kenworthy 2022 (AJPH), not by a 2024 Kenworthy-AND-Igra co-authored item. So "Kenworthy and Igra 2024" mis-pairs an author and a year: it is either Igra & Kenworthy 2022 (the empirical paper) or Kenworthy 2024 (the solo book). "Ly and Soman 2020" could NOT be verified as an existing source at all. Separately, the same sentence's "Berliner and Kenworthy's 2017 study documented over 250,000 campaigns annually... 25-40% of stated need" is itself shaky: Berliner & Kenworthy 2017 analyzed 200 campaigns (found ~90% missed goal); the 250,000+/437,000 scale figures come from the later Igra/Kenworthy work and GoFundMe's own reporting.
recommendation: FIX THE CITES, KEEP THE CLAIM (the underlying pattern is well supported). (1) Replace "Kenworthy and Igra 2024" with the precise pairing that matches the asserted content: "(Igra and Kenworthy 2022; Kenworthy 2024)" — Igra & Kenworthy, AJPH 112(3):491-498 (2022) for the 437,000-campaign scale + 12%-met-goal + disparity-mirroring finding, and Kenworthy, Crowded Out (MIT Press, 2024) for the synthesis. (2) For "Ly and Soman 2020": author/editor must EITHER supply the exact reference (full title + journal/venue) if one genuinely exists in their notes, OR drop it. It is currently unverifiable and is the weakest link; do not ship as-is. Medical Anthropology Quarterly 35(4) (2021), or Saleh et al. on racial/gender disparities in successful campaigns (SSM 2023). (3) BONUS (adjacent line, flag to author): re-anchor the "over 250,000 campaigns annually / 25-40% of need" figures — Berliner & Kenworthy 2017 is a 200-campaign qualitative study and does not support those scale numbers; move the scale figures to GoFundMe's published annual reporting and/or Igra & Kenworthy 2022.
